notes: No named opposition group found. A Change.org petition titled 'Stop the construction of a data center in Plainfield, Indiana' was launched. Over a dozen residents opposed the project at public comment. Plainfield Plan Commission approved the project unanimously despite opposition.

No named opposition group found. The moratorium passed unanimously without debate or public comments. Concerns were raised by supervisors and constituents about water pollution, power needs, and location impacts. The moratorium was proactive rather than driven by organized grassroots opposition.

No named opposition group found. Resident opposition centered on noise complaints from CyrusOne's existing data center on Diehl Road near I-88. Residents described fan noise as 'like constant traffic' and backup generator noise as 'unlivable'. Aurora City Council approved a six-month moratorium in response. The city reached a legal agreement with CyrusOne including noise mitigation measures.

No formal named opposition group found. Phyllis Elizabeth Wall brought a separate paper petition with 150+ signatures to a board meeting. A Change.org petition 'Stop the Foristell Data Center Project' gathered 548 signatures. Over 100 residents attempted to attend a board of aldermen meeting, forcing postponement to a larger venue.

No named opposition group found. Community opposition was driven by concerns over the project's secrecy (officials signed NDAs with Spark Innovations, linked to Google) and impacts on water and energy. The St. Charles City Council unanimously passed a one-year moratorium in August 2025 and is now considering a permanent ban on data centers.

No formal named opposition group, but residents organized around a 'No Eminent Domain' campaign, wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts and carrying signs reading 'My land, my choice' at packed city meetings. Leon Westerhausen was a spokesperson for the opposition. Concerns focused on eminent domain, power consumption, and land development impacts.

No formal named opposition group found. Two Change.org petitions exist: 'Stop Data Centers in Cumberland County' (1,000+ signatures) and 'Stop the Data Center in Cumberland County' (293 signatures). Opposition focuses on Energy Storage Solutions' proposed 'NetZero' data center which would rely on natural gas. Fayetteville City Council passed a 90-day moratorium. ESS sued Tarboro after its town council rejected an almost identical proposal.

No named opposition group found. Gates County was the first county in North Carolina to enact a one-year data center moratorium. Residents stepped forward one by one to voice strong opposition, with many urging commissioners to go further and enact a permanent ban. Concerns included wildlife impacts, water consumption, noise pollution, lowering of property values, and environmental injustice.

MountainTrue (environmental nonprofit) and the Southern Environmental Law Center sent a letter supporting Canton's moratorium. About 200 residents gathered at the public hearing. Canton Town Council voted unanimously for a 12-month moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining. Only 2 of the speakers opposed the moratorium. MountainTrue emphasized protecting the Pigeon River's biodiversity.

No named opposition group found. Kings Mountain City Council voted 5-2 for a six-month moratorium on new data center projects. Residents raised concerns about utility cost increases (one resident's power bill went from $150 to $500), water quality issues, and infrastructure impact. The two dissenting council members wanted to wait a month before starting the pause, not because they opposed it.

No named opposition group found. Boone is part of the broader wave of North Carolina communities passing data center moratoriums. Limited specific information found about organized groups in Boone itself.

No formal named opposition group, but Kelly Boegemann organized online petitions and in-person opposition efforts against 'Project Mustang' (Beale Infrastructure). She started a petition to keep data centers out of Rogers County before even knowing about Project Mustang. Petition gathered hundreds of signatures. Darren Blanchard was arrested at a public meeting for speaking ~30 seconds over his allotted time. More than 20 speakers voiced opposition at a 3-hour meeting.

No named opposition group found. Jerome Township became the first local government in Ohio to impose a data center moratorium (9 months, September 2025). The moratorium was driven by strong and widespread community opposition. Residents raised concerns about constant noise pollution ('industrial buzz'), electricity and water consumption driving up utility costs, minimal jobs, and strain on emergency services. The Jerome Fire Chief warned local fire departments struggle to manage calls to existing Amazon data centers.

No named opposition group found. Gene Krebs, a former Republican state lawmaker from Preble County, helped lead the opposition. Residents organized a referendum campaign that led developer Patrick Scanlon to withdraw his rezoning application in October. The planning commission had already denied the proposal 11-0. Krebs said he'd never seen citizens so 'universally upset and perturbed' about an issue.

No formal named opposition group, but the movement was led by local resident Cassia Core through a Change.org petition that gathered nearly 2,000 signatures. Opposition centers on the proposed conversion of the IX Center (which hosted the Cleveland Auto Show and Christmas Connection) into an AI data center. Concerns include noise pollution, utility price increases, and loss of the community venue.

No named opposition group found. Approximately 150 residents stormed a Wood County Planning Commission meeting in January 2026 demanding answers about the Meta data center and requesting a moratorium on future data centers. Residents were angered that non-disclosure agreements kept the Meta name secret even after construction began. A 350 MW natural gas power plant (Apollo Power Generation Facility) was approved by the Ohio Power Siting Board with no public hearings, further enraging residents.

No specific named opposition group found for Sunbury. Dozens of residents packed public meetings to speak out against the proposed Amazon data center that would rezone 330+ acres for industrial use. Online organizing on Facebook (including the broader 'Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio' group) has led to protests and distribution of 'Stop Data Center' signs. Public hearing was postponed and rescheduled for March 23, 2026.

No named opposition group found. Blanchester Village Council unanimously approved a moratorium on data center development (Ordinance 2026.007) on February 12, 2026. The moratorium was preemptive, following data center proposals in nearby Wilmington and Mount Orab. Council members cited concerns about better opportunities for Blanchester than data centers.

Better Path Coalition (co-founded by Karen Feridun) is a nonprofit that advocates for clean energy and opposes data centers in Pennsylvania. A Change.org petition 'Oppose data centers in Linfield and Limerick' gathered 680+ signatures. The Limerick Township Board of Supervisors voted to send a letter opposing a state land swap that could enable data center development. State Sen. Katie Muth warned the swap could push the region toward becoming 'Data Center Alley 2.0'. Despite opposition, the PA Game Commission approved the controversial land swap.

Prevent North Coventry Data Center is a Facebook group organized by local residents including Paula Terrell. North Coventry Township supervisors voted 3-2 to reject a proposed 120,000 sq ft data center by Envision Land Use. The developer confirmed the proposal will not proceed due to public opposition. Residents are now advocating for the township to adopt a local data center ordinance. The group also signed a statement opposing PA House Bill 2151.

No named opposition group found. Air Products proposed a 2.6 million sq ft data center complex at its former headquarters. A zoning hearing was continued to a later date after dozens of residents showed up. The Lehigh Valley Planning Commission raised concerns about noise from cooling equipment, exhaust, and nighttime lighting impacts on the surrounding neighborhood. Part of a broader wave of grassroots opposition across Pennsylvania spanning political lines.

No named opposition group found. For more than two hours, 25 speakers addressed Dickson City council, all either raising concerns or outright opposing data centers. County Controller Gary DiBileo called data centers 'water-sucking, energy-draining, noise-making, environmentally damaging monstrosities.' County Commissioner Bill Gaughan questioned 'progress for whom and at what cost' and planned to send a formal letter to Gov. Shapiro requesting a statewide moratorium. Council passed a zoning ordinance restricting data centers to light manufacturing districts.

Protect Cumberland County PA (PCCPA) is the primary organized opposition group, with a dedicated website tracking the $15B Powerhouse Data Centers / PA Data Center Partners project (also known as Project Bolt / Pennsylvania Digital I). The Appalachian Trail Conservancy also identified concerns. Hundreds attended meetings, and residents voiced frustration over transparency and environmental risks. The project was fast-tracked through a zoning overlay amendment.

No named opposition group found specific to East Whiteland, though the Better Path Coalition (Karen Feridun) is active across Pennsylvania data center fights. A planning commission meeting was postponed after too many residents showed up, exceeding venue capacity. Sentinel Data Centers wants to expand a previously approved project by 61% to exceed 1.6 million sq ft on a remediated Superfund site. Residents of nearby Malvern Hunt (~280 homes) worry about noise, well water safety, and health risks from digging at the Superfund site.

No formal named opposition group, but Amanda Doughty, a lifelong Peninsula resident, created a Change.org petition that gathered over 4,000 signatures. The petition went from 100 signatures within 24 hours to over 3,500 within a week. The petition forced city leaders to clarify that no concrete data center plans exist. Concerns included environmental impact, health impacts, and increased utility bills.

No formal named group, but residents of New Market Village organized strongly, attending meetings wearing matching 'No Data Centers' shirts and holding signs. The Henrico Board of Zoning Appeals denied Centra Logistics' vested-rights appeal for a 1 million sq ft data center complex on Darbytown Road. Board of Supervisors chair Roscoe Cooper spoke against the project, saying 'For too long, Eastern Henrico has been inundated with the industrial and manufacturing weight of the county.' The decision drew cheers from dozens of neighbors.

Limited specific information found about organized opposition groups in Mount Horeb. Mount Horeb is listed among Wisconsin communities (with Mount Pleasant, Beaver Dam, Port Washington, Janesville) that have rallied against data center approvals. The broader Wisconsin movement uses the slogan 'Dairyland, not Data Land.' Democratic state lawmakers proposed a statewide data center construction moratorium in February 2026.

No named opposition group found. Weld County commissioners are proactively proposing a zoning ordinance to define and regulate data centers, keeping them within industrial or agricultural zones with site plan or special review requirements. Residents raised concerns about water usage, noise, and infrastructure demands. Global AI is in early stages of developing a data center on the former Carestream Health (Kodak) site between Windsor and Greeley, with potential investment of $2B-$20B.

No named opposition group found. Hundreds of residents packed Pacific High School for the planning and zoning meeting. The developer (Beltline Energy, Atlanta-based) withdrew its $16B rezoning application, citing inability to finalize a funding agreement. The Missouri Department of Conservation flagged the 500-acre site near the Meramec River for habitat of endangered bats and salamanders. Residents in the unincorporated area rely on wells for water and feared the facility would drain them.

No named opposition group found. The Phillipsburg Town Council unanimously voted to ban data centers anywhere within the town's borders, citing environmental, water, and energy concerns. The ban aims to protect the town's rural character and agricultural lands. This is part of a broader trend in New Jersey where towns are pushing back against data center development.
